title: What Comes Next: Autonomous Systems, EW, and Distributed Force Design

source.original: General Valerii Zaluzhnyi on the Impossibility of Operational-Level Tasks Due to Technological Progress — Giorgi Revishvili Thread

body_html:
If Zaluzhnyi's diagnosis is correct — that technology has killed operational-level maneuver — then the prescription for future force design becomes clearer, if no less difficult. Militaries must invest heavily in electronic warfare, autonomous systems, and distributed architectures that can survive and fight effectively on a transparent battlefield.

Electronic warfare becomes paramount because the kill web depends on electromagnetic connectivity. Disrupting the sensor-to-shooter links that enable precision fires and persistent ISR can create temporary windows of opacity — moments where forces can move, reposition, or concentrate without immediate detection. EW is no longer a supporting capability; it is a precondition for any operational activity.

Autonomous systems — from uncrewed aerial vehicles to ground robots and AI-enabled decision aids — offer a way to generate combat mass without concentrating vulnerable human formations. Swarms of cheap, expendable drones can saturate defenses, conduct reconnaissance, and deliver effects without presenting the high-value targets that precision fires are optimized to destroy.

Distributed force design means abandoning the assumption that combat power must be concentrated at a single point of decision. Instead, forces operate in smaller, dispersed elements that are individually less detectable and collectively more resilient. Command and control must become more decentralized, with junior leaders empowered to act within commander's intent rather than waiting for orders from centralized headquarters that are themselves targets.

None of these shifts are easy. They require not just new equipment but new organizational cultures, training paradigms, and doctrinal frameworks. The militaries that adapt fastest to the realities Zaluzhnyi describes will hold a decisive advantage. Those that cling to the maneuver warfare paradigm risk preparing exquisitely for a war that no longer exists.
